The payoff comes in Layers 2-4.

docs.title: Layer 2: Capture Everything
docs.text:
Tool: Granola (meeting transcription) + Claude Code

Once the project folder exists, the next step is to stop losing information. Meetings are where most decisions happen and most context gets lost.

What this does:

  • Granola transcribes all meetings — Zoom, in-person, phone calls
  • Claude Code fetches transcripts and archives them in the project folder
  • Decisions, action items, and context are preserved automatically
  • Meetings stop being ephemeral

The shift: Before transcription, meetings were a black hole. Something would be decided, an action item assigned, and three weeks later nobody could remember the details. Now every meeting is searchable. The weekly review (Layer 3) mines these transcripts automatically.

Before you start

Requires: Granola installed (free tier works). A transcripts folder in your project structure. Time: 5 minutes to set up. Zero ongoing effort — Granola runs automatically.

docs.title: Layer 3: The Weekly Review
docs.text:
Skill: /weekly-review [PM]

This is where the system comes alive. The weekly review pulls from every source and produces two outputs: a high-level dashboard and a detailed weekly log.

What it does:

  • Collects data from meeting transcripts, all project-related emails, and working documents from Google Docs — plus WhatsApp group chats if you have that integration configured
  • Synthesizes everything into a project dashboard (strategic objectives, operational status, team to-dos, critical success factors)
  • Produces a detailed weekly log with thematic analysis across all sources
  • Updates the Google Doc hub directly — no copy-pasting
  • Gracefully degrades if any source is unavailable (WhatsApp down? It skips it and notes the gap)

What the dashboard looks like:

The dashboard prioritizes strategic objectives first, then operational objectives that serve them, then everyone's to-do items and critical success factors. It evolved over time to answer the question: What are the strategic goals? How do this week's tasks serve them? What absolutely must happen?

The real value: Week-to-week accountability. Meeting prep that takes seconds instead of hours. A team that stays focused because the review surfaces what matters. New team members who can read two months of weekly reviews and understand the project's trajectory.

How the Google Doc formatting works

Claude Code formats your Google Doc programmatically via the API — applying heading styles (H1 for major sections, H2 for subsections, H3 for person names and meeting topics), bold for team member names, and italic for metadata. This means the dashboard structure is consistent every time the weekly review runs.

The first time takes some back-and-forth. When you set up a new project's Google Doc, expect a collaborative process: Claude applies the formatting it can, you review the result and flag what needs adjusting, and Claude refines. Some formatting — like adjusting spacing, tweaking font sizes for emphasis, or fixing paragraph breaks where content ran together — is easier to do manually in the Google Doc than to program via the API. This is normal. Think of the first formatting pass as calibration.

After the first time, the structure persists. When /weekly-review overwrites the dashboard each week, it applies the same heading hierarchy and formatting rules. The Google Doc retains its structure across updates. You shouldn't need to reformat manually after the initial setup — though you can always tweak if your preferences change.

What Claude handles vs. what you handle:

Claude (automatic) You (manual, first time only) Heading styles (H1-H3) for all section headers Fine-tuning spacing between sections Bold for team member names throughout Adjusting any paragraph breaks that merged during import Italic for dates and metadata lines Custom colors or highlighting for emphasis Consistent structure across weekly updates Tab creation (Claude can't create tabs via API)

Before you start

Requires: Layer 1 (project folder + config) complete. Google Docs MCP. Gmail MCP. WhatsApp MCP optional. Granola optional. Time: First run takes 30-60 minutes (fetching and processing all sources). Subsequent runs: 10-20 minutes.

How this evolved

The first version was a wall of text. Too much detail, no prioritization. Over three weeks I iterated: What should the dashboard emphasize? What's noise vs. signal? The breakthrough was organizing around strategic priorities rather than chronological events. I also had to tune the email filtering — too broad and you get newsletters mixed in, too narrow and you miss important threads from collaborators. The skill got better every week as I corrected it.

See what this produces — full synthetic example

The example below uses a fictional project ("Kingston Youth Futures" — a youth violence prevention RCT in Jamaica) to show the actual structure and level of detail that /weekly-review generates. The project, team, and data are invented. The format is real.

Tab 1: Project Overview & Meeting Log — the dashboard that opens every meeting:

Tab 2: Research Design & Progress — the living research design that proposals draw from:

Full example — Tab 1 (Project Overview) Full example — Tab 2 (Research Design) View as Google Doc

docs.title: Layer 4: Proposals and Reports on Demand
docs.text:
Skills: /proposal-write and /proposal-revise [PM]

Because the Google Doc hub contains the research design, project history, all prior proposals (successful, unsuccessful, pending), and the weekly review keeps everything current — Claude Code can draft almost anything.

What these do:

  • /proposal-write drafts funding proposals, LOIs, donor summary reports, status updates, and abstracts
  • Takes an input checklist (funder, template, budget, deadline) and auto-gathers context from the project record
  • Looks up donor profiles to tailor framing
  • For resubmissions: categorizes reviewer comments and maps responses to sections
  • /proposal-revise applies feedback from collaborators, reviewers, or self-review
  • Both enforce voice consistency through a configurable voice pack

How it works in practice: A collaborator reads the draft, dictates their feedback in one stream, and Claude extracts the actionable items and applies them. Nobody edits markdown directly. The revision skill tracks changes, backs up the previous version, and reports what moved.

Quality assurance: After drafting or revising, two agent-based reviewers provide independent critique:

  • Writing Reviewer — evaluates clarity, argument structure, and voice consistency
  • Methodology Reviewer — checks causal language, identification strategy, and statistical practice

These run as subprocesses in fresh context, so they catch things the main session misses.

Before you start

Requires: Layers 1-3 working. A voice pack with your writing style (template provided). Prior proposals in the project folder help significantly. Time: First proposal draft: 30-60 minutes (including context gathering). Revisions: 10-20 minutes.

docs.title: The Key Insights
docs.text: Two things matter more than any individual skill:

docs.title: Each layer builds on the previous one
docs.text:
You don't need to build all four at once. Start with organizing your folders. Add transcription when you're ready. The weekly review only works because the folder structure exists. The proposal skills only work because the weekly review keeps everything current.

graph TD
    L0["Layer 0: Digital Cleanup<br/><small>Optional one-time reset</small>"]
    L1["Layer 1: Project Folder<br/><small>Foundation — works alone</small>"]
    L2["Layer 2: Transcription<br/><small>Captures meetings</small>"]
    L3["Layer 3: Weekly Review<br/><small>Synthesizes everything</small>"]
    L4["Layer 4: Proposals & Reports<br/><small>Produces deliverables</small>"]

    L0 -.->|optional| L1
    L1 --> L2
    L1 --> L3
    L2 --> L3
    L1 --> L4
    L2 --> L4
    L3 --> L4

    style L0 stroke-dasharray: 5 5

Start anywhere. Layer 1 works on its own. Each additional layer builds on what came before — but you don't need all four to get value.

docs.title: Every skill took about three weeks of iteration
docs.text: None of these worked perfectly on day one. Each one required back-and-forth with Claude Code — adapting it to specific projects, adjusting what the weekly review emphasized, tuning the proposal voice, figuring out what the team actually needed. The downloadable skills are a starting point, not a finished product. You will customize them to your workflow.

docs.title: Versatility
docs.text: This system was developed primarily for quantitative research projects (randomized trials, survey-based studies), but it adapted to qualitative projects with modest adjustments. The core structure — organized folders, captured meetings, weekly reviews, document generation — works across project types. What you iterate on is the specifics: what the weekly review highlights, what the proposal skills pull from, how the dashboard is structured.

docs.title: Get the Skills
docs.text:
All skills referenced above are available for download:

Skill What it does Install /setup-project-management Interactive project folder setup Details /weekly-review Multi-source weekly synthesis Details /proposal-write Draft proposals from project context Details /proposal-revise Apply feedback to drafts Details Writing Reviewer Academic prose QA (agent) Details Methodology Reviewer Empirical methods QA (agent) Details

docs.title: Nairobi Digital Futures — Project Overview & Meeting Log
docs.text:
This is a synthetic example showing what a /weekly-review produces over time. The project, team, and data are entirely fictional — names, institutions, and details do not correspond to any real study. The structure and level of detail are realistic.

=== PROJECT STATUS DASHBOARD === Last updated: 2026-02-14 (Week of Feb 10-14) Updated by: Claude Code /weekly-review

STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE (NORTH STAR) Estimate the causal impact of a digital financial literacy + mobile savings program ("Jipange") on savings behavior, income, and economic empowerment among women market vendors in Nairobi, Kenya — and identify which program components drive the effects.

STRATEGIC PRIORITIES 1. Complete Cohort 2 screening and randomization by March 15 2. Maintain Cohort 1 training attendance above 80% 3. Finalize midline survey instrument and pilot by end of February

CRITICAL SUCCESS FACTORS

  • Market association trust and cooperation in all three market areas Status: GREEN Owner: Faith Wambui Note: Gikomba Market association confirmed continued cooperation after Jan community meeting. Kawangware relationship solid. Eastleigh requires ongoing engagement — new market chair elected in January.

  • Financial capability screening tool validity and completion rate Status: GREEN Owner: Lisa Park Note: Cohort 1 screening completion rate 94%. Adapted FINDEX-based assessment performing well. Two items flagged for cultural adaptation review (see Research Design doc).

  • Training fidelity and facilitator quality Status: YELLOW Owner: James Mwangi Note: Average session attendance 76% (target 80%). Two of six trainers below threshold. Remediation plan in place — additional training session scheduled Feb 20. One trainer (Eastleigh site B) may need replacement.

  • Ethics compliance across institutions Status: GREEN Owner: Sarah Okonkwo Note: Georgetown IRB renewal approved through Dec 2026. University of Nairobi ethics committee renewal submitted Jan 30, expected approval by Feb 28. No adverse events reported.

  • Cohort enrollment targets Status: GREEN Owner: Lisa Park Note: Cohort 1 enrolled 380/400 target (95%). Cohort 2 screening underway with 290 of ~400 screened. On track.

  • Gates Foundation reporting and relationship Status: GREEN Owner: Sarah Okonkwo Note: Q1 progress report submitted Feb 1. Program officer (Amanda Chen) positive on enrollment numbers. Site visit tentatively scheduled for April.

WHERE WE ARE

Cohort 1 (n=380) is nine weeks into the 12-week Jipange program. Training group attendance averaging 76%, slightly below our 80% target, concentrated in two trainer groups. We have a remediation plan. Control group contamination checks show no evidence of spillover — the market-cluster randomization is holding.

Cohort 2 screening is 73% complete (290/~400). We're running two screening teams across Eastleigh and Kawangware simultaneously. Gikomba screening starts next week. Randomization planned for March 15 pending screening completion.

The midline survey instrument is in second-round revision. James flagged concerns about the self-reported income module — response rates on sensitive items were low in the Cohort 1 baseline. We're testing list experiment and indirect question alternatives for the midline.

Budget is on track. Gates Foundation Q1 disbursement received. IDRC supplemental ($120K) confirmed but not yet disbursed — expected March.

COHORT STATUS SUMMARY

Cohort 1 (Training Phase) Enrolled: 380 (target 400) Treatment arm: 192 | Control arm: 188 Market clusters: 6 treatment, 6 control Program start: Dec 9, 2025 Program week: 9 of 12 Avg attendance (treatment): 76% Baseline survey complete: Yes (response rate 97%) Next milestone: Midline survey (Apr 2026)

Cohort 2 (Screening Phase) Screened to date: 290 of ~400 Screening start: Jan 20, 2026 Screening teams: 2 active Target randomization: Mar 15, 2026 Market clusters: 6 (3 Eastleigh, 2 Kawangware, 1 Gikomba)

Cohort 3 (Planning Phase) Target enrollment: ~400 Screening start: Jun 2026 Market clusters: 6 (remaining eligible clusters) Status: Market mapping underway

KEY METRICS

Cohort 1 training attendance: 76% (target 80%) Cohort 1 baseline response rate: 97% Cohort 2 screening completion: 73% (290/~400) Trainer quality (above threshold): 4 of 6 Budget burn rate: 23% of total ($189K of $820K) Adverse events: 0 Market association incidents: 1 (minor, resolved — see Jan 28 meeting log)

TIMELINE AND UPCOMING MILESTONES

Feb 17-21: Cohort 2 Gikomba Market screening begins Feb 20: Trainer remediation session (James + trainers) Feb 24-28: Midline survey instrument pilot (n=30 from Cohort 1 control) Mar 1: IDRC supplemental disbursement (expected) Mar 15: Cohort 2 randomization Mar 24: Cohort 2 program launch Apr 7-25: Cohort 1 midline survey Apr (TBD): Gates Foundation site visit Jun 2026: Cohort 3 screening begins Aug 2026: Cohort 1 endline survey

CURRENT PRIORITIES BY PERSON

Sarah Okonkwo (PI) - Review midline survey instrument revision (due Feb 18) - Prepare for Gates Foundation site visit logistics (April) - Finalize power calculations memo with updated ICC estimates - Review Cohort 2 randomization protocol with Lisa

James Mwangi (Co-PI) - Lead trainer remediation session (Feb 20) - Evaluate trainer replacement decision for Eastleigh site B - Finalize midline self-reported income module (list experiment vs. indirect questions) - Supervise qualitative interviews with Cohort 1 participants (ongoing)

Lisa Park (Research Manager) - Complete Cohort 2 screening (target: Mar 10) - Prepare randomization protocol and pre-registration update - Manage screening team logistics for Gikomba Market - Compile Cohort 1 attendance data for trainer performance review

Faith Wambui (Field Researcher) - Coordinate Gikomba Market association entry for Cohort 2 screening - Continue market mapping for Cohort 3 clusters - Follow up with new Eastleigh market chair (relationship building)

Priya Sharma (RA) - Lead midline survey piloting (Feb 24-28) - Enter and clean Cohort 2 screening data (ongoing) - Prepare list experiment programming in SurveyCTO

Daniel Ochieng (RA) - Manage Cohort 1 attendance tracking and trainer performance data - Prepare training fidelity report for James's review - Assist with Cohort 2 screening in Kawangware

Grace Njeri (RA) - Process Cohort 1 qualitative interview transcripts - Assist Priya with midline pilot logistics - Update participant tracking database

KEY CHALLENGES

  1. Training fidelity below target. Two trainers below 80% attendance threshold. If remediation doesn't work, we need replacement trainers — which means retraining and potentially losing 2-3 weeks of Cohort 1 program time. James leading this.

  2. Sensitive survey items. Self-reported income and savings measures had low response rates at baseline (68% on the most sensitive items). Need a measurement solution before midline. List experiments add survey length but may be necessary.

  3. Eastleigh market leadership transition. New market chair elected January — doesn't know the project. Faith is building the relationship but it's early. This affects Cohort 2 screening in Eastleigh and eventually Cohort 3.

  4. IDRC disbursement timing. The $120K supplemental is confirmed but disbursement is slow. We're covering Cohort 2 screening costs from the Gates budget, but need the IDRC funds before Cohort 3 to avoid a gap.

FUNDING PIPELINE

Gates Foundation (primary): $700K over 3 years. Q1 disbursement received ($175K). On track. IDRC (supplemental): $120K. Approved, disbursement expected March 2026. Total budget: $820K. Spent to date: $189K (23%). No new funding applications pending. Potential: Ford Foundation expression of interest for qualitative component add-on ($80K). Sarah to discuss with James.

PARTNER AND STAKEHOLDER STATUS

Kenya National Bureau of Statistics: Engaged. Director briefed Jan 15. Supportive. Requesting quarterly updates. Kenya Women's Enterprise Fund: Active partner. Providing community trainers and referral network. Monthly coordination calls. University of Nairobi (Department of Economics): James's home institution. Hosting field office. Ethics committee renewal pending. IPA Kenya: Lisa's employer. Managing field operations. Contract renewed through Dec 2026. Gates Foundation: Positive. Program officer Amanda Chen responsive. Site visit April.

DECISIONS MADE THIS WEEK

  1. Midline survey will include list experiment module for sensitive income items (replacing direct self-report). James and Priya to finalize design by Feb 21.

  2. Trainer remediation before replacement. Will assess after Feb 20 training. Decision on replacement by Mar 1.

  3. Cohort 2 randomization will proceed March 15 even if screening falls slightly short of 400 target. Minimum threshold: 360.

  4. Power calculations memo will use updated ICC estimates from Cohort 1 baseline data (ICC = 0.048, lower than assumed 0.05). Sarah to circulate by Feb 21.

SHORT-TERM TASKS (Next 2 Weeks)

[ ] Sarah: Review midline instrument (Feb 18) [ ] Sarah: Circulate updated power calc memo (Feb 21) [ ] James: Lead trainer remediation session (Feb 20) [ ] James: Finalize list experiment design (Feb 21) [ ] Lisa: Complete Cohort 2 screening (Mar 10) [ ] Lisa: Draft randomization protocol (Feb 28) [ ] Faith: Gikomba Market coordination for screening (Feb 17) [ ] Priya: Program list experiment in SurveyCTO (Feb 24) [ ] Priya: Run midline pilot (Feb 24-28) [ ] Daniel: Compile trainer attendance report (Feb 19) [ ] Grace: Complete qualitative transcript batch 3 (Feb 21)

=== END DASHBOARD ===

docs.title: Meeting: Nairobi Digital Futures Weekly Check-In
docs.text:
Date: 2026-02-12 (Wednesday), 10:00 AM EAT Participants: Sarah Okonkwo, James Mwangi, Lisa Park, Faith Wambui, Daniel Ochieng (via Zoom) Duration: 58 minutes Source: Granola transcript

Note: This summary was generated from a meeting transcript by Claude Code. It captures key discussions and decisions but may not reflect every comment or nuance. Team members should flag any corrections.

docs.title: 1. Cohort 1 Training Fidelity Review
docs.text:
Daniel presented the latest attendance data. Overall training group attendance is 76%, down slightly from 78% two weeks ago. The decline is concentrated in two trainer groups:

  • Trainer Group 3 (Eastleigh site B): 64% average attendance. The trainer, Rose, has been inconsistent with session scheduling and two participants reported feeling the sessions "weren't relevant." James noted this is the same trainer flagged in the January review. He recommended we proceed with a replacement rather than another round of coaching.

  • Trainer Group 5 (Kawangware site A): 71% average attendance. This appears to be a transportation issue rather than trainer quality — the session venue changed in January and several participants have longer travel times. Lisa is looking into a venue closer to the original location.

The other four groups are all above 82%, with Group 1 (Eastleigh site A) at 89% — the highest in the study.

Decision: James will lead one more remediation training on Feb 20 that includes all trainers (not just underperformers, to avoid stigma). If Group 3 attendance doesn't improve to 72% by Mar 1, we replace Rose. The Kenya Women's Enterprise Fund has two backup trainers who completed training in December but weren't assigned.

Sarah raised the question of whether we need to document the trainer variation for the paper. James agreed it's important — differential training intensity by trainer is a potential source of heterogeneity. Daniel will compile a trainer-level attendance dataset that we can use in analysis. Lisa noted we should also track session content fidelity, not just attendance — whether trainers are covering all module components. James will add a fidelity checklist to the observation protocol.

docs.title: 2. Cohort 2 Screening Progress
docs.text:
Lisa reported 290 screened out of a target of approximately 400. Two screening teams are running in parallel — one in Eastleigh (led by Priya) and one in Kawangware (led by Grace, with Daniel assisting remotely on data entry).

Eastleigh screening is slightly behind schedule. Lisa attributed this to the market leadership transition — the new chair, Mrs. Oduya, initially wanted to "review the project" before allowing continued access. Faith met with her twice and it's now resolved, but they lost about four days.

Gikomba Market screening starts Feb 17. Faith confirmed the market association liaison (Mrs. Kamau) is ready and has identified the screening venue — the same community hall used for Cohort 1. Faith noted one concern: a recent dispute between vendor associations in the area (Feb 5) has increased tensions. She recommended waiting until the following week to start if tensions remain elevated. The group discussed and agreed Faith should make a game-day decision based on local conditions.

Lisa presented the screening data quality so far. The adapted FINDEX-based financial capability assessment is performing well — completion rate 94%, average screening time 35 minutes. Two items flagged for review:

  • Item 14 (informal borrowing): High refusal rate (12%). Women seem reluctant to discuss borrowing from informal lenders. Priya suggested rewording to ask about "people who help you when money is short" rather than "lenders" — less stigmatizing.

  • Item 22 (savings amounts): Possible social desirability bias — reported savings seem high compared to national survey data for this income group. James suggested adding a validation cross-check with M-Pesa records at midline, but noted it adds complexity.

Decision: Keep current screening tool as-is for Cohort 2 (consistency with Cohort 1). Flag items 14 and 22 for revision before Cohort 3. Lisa will document the concerns in the screening protocol memo.

docs.title: 3. Midline Survey Design
docs.text:
James presented the revised midline instrument (v2.1). The major change is the proposed switch from direct self-report to list experiments for the three most sensitive financial items:

  1. "In the past 3 months, have you borrowed from an informal money lender?"
  2. "In the past 3 months, have you missed a loan repayment?"
  3. "In the past 3 months, have you hidden income or savings from your household?"

At baseline, these items had response rates of 78%, 65%, and 68% respectively — well below the 95%+ response rate on non-sensitive items. James walked through the list experiment design:

  • Control list: 4 innocuous items (e.g., "visited a health clinic," "attended a market association meeting")
  • Treatment list: Same 4 items + 1 sensitive item
  • Randomly assign half of respondents to each list per item
  • Need ~200 per list per item for adequate power

Sarah raised two concerns. First, list experiments sacrifice individual-level measurement — we get prevalence estimates but can't link responses to individual treatment effects. This limits heterogeneity analysis. James acknowledged this but argued that biased direct reports are worse than imprecise list experiments. He proposed a combined design: direct self-report first (for those willing), then list experiment for everyone. We can compare the two and use the list experiment to correct for bias.

Second concern: survey length. The current instrument is 45 minutes. Adding list experiments for three items adds approximately 8 minutes. James noted we could cut the time-use module (10 minutes) to compensate — it was included for the descriptive paper but isn't essential for the main experimental estimates.

Decision: Proceed with combined design (direct report + list experiment). Cut the detailed time-use module; keep the abbreviated version (3 items instead of 12). Priya will program the list experiments in SurveyCTO and pilot Feb 24-28 with 30 control group participants from Cohort 1.

docs.title: 4. Partner Coordination and Upcoming Events
docs.text:
Sarah updated the team on the Gates Foundation Q1 report, submitted Feb 1. Amanda Chen (program officer) responded positively, particularly about the enrollment numbers and market engagement strategy. She mentioned a possible site visit in April — no date confirmed yet. Sarah will follow up to nail down timing so we can prepare.

James reported that the University of Nairobi ethics committee renewal is pending. He submitted Jan 30 and expects approval by end of February. No issues anticipated — this is a routine annual renewal with no protocol changes.

Faith raised the question of the Kenya National Bureau of Statistics quarterly update. The last briefing was Jan 15. The next one should be in April, ideally timed with the Gates visit if possible so Sarah only needs to travel once. James offered to present the qualitative findings at the KNBS briefing to add depth beyond the quantitative enrollment numbers.

Lisa flagged that the IPA Kenya contract renewal went through. Her position is secure through Dec 2026. She also noted that IPA's regional director (Michael Torres) will be in Nairobi in March and wants to visit the field sites. Lisa will coordinate.

Action items from this discussion: - Sarah to follow up with Amanda Chen on April site visit timing - James to coordinate with KNBS for April briefing - Lisa to schedule Michael Torres field visit (March) - Faith to assess Gikomba Market situation before Feb 17 screening launch

docs.title: Meeting: Nairobi Digital Futures — Midline Design Working Session
docs.text:
Date: 2026-02-05 (Wednesday), 2:00 PM EAT Participants: Sarah Okonkwo, James Mwangi, Priya Sharma (via Zoom) Duration: 42 minutes Source: Granola transcript

Note: This summary was generated from a meeting transcript by Claude Code. It captures key discussions and decisions but may not reflect every comment or nuance. Team members should flag any corrections.

docs.title: 1. Baseline Data Review for Midline Planning
docs.text:
Sarah opened with baseline summary statistics from Cohort 1. Key findings relevant to midline design:

  • Balance check: Treatment and control groups well-balanced on all 14 baseline covariates. Largest normalized difference is 0.08 SD (household income). No concerns.
  • Income and savings measures: As noted above, direct self-report items on borrowing and savings had 65-78% response rates. Non-sensitive items averaged 96%. The gap confirms measurement concerns.
  • Financial capability scores: The adapted FINDEX assessment classified 31% of the sample as low-capability, 45% as moderate, and 24% as high. This distribution is close to what we expected based on the pilot data from 2024.
  • Attrition concern: 6 participants (1.6%) unreachable at the 4-week check-in. All in control group. Lisa is following up with market association contacts. Not yet concerning but worth monitoring.

docs.title: 2. List Experiment Technical Design
docs.text:
Priya presented three design options for the list experiments. After discussion, the group settled on the double list approach — each respondent sees both a short list (3 items) and a long list (4 items, one sensitive), randomly assigned to which list is short vs. long. This approach:

  • Reduces floor/ceiling effects
  • Allows internal consistency checks
  • Adds minimal time vs. single list

Priya estimated programming time: 2 weeks in SurveyCTO, including randomization logic and built-in quality checks. The pilot on Feb 24-28 will test comprehension, timing, and enumerator comfort.

docs.title: 3. Endorsement Experiment Alternative
docs.text:
James raised endorsement experiments as an alternative for the informal lending item specifically. Rather than asking about own borrowing, the endorsement experiment asks about support for policies or organizations associated with informal lending regulation. The idea: the program may shift attitudes toward formal financial services even if participants won't report their own borrowing behavior.

Sarah liked the idea conceptually but was concerned about interpretation — endorsement experiments measure attitudes, not behavior, and our primary outcomes are behavioral. The group agreed to include one endorsement experiment item on attitudes toward formal vs. informal financial services as a secondary outcome but not replace the list experiment.

Decision: Primary sensitive measurement via list experiments. Add one endorsement experiment item on financial service attitudes as secondary outcome. Priya to program both.

The project, team, and data are entirely fictional — names, institutions, and details do not correspond to any real study. The structure and level of detail are realistic.

docs.title: North Star
docs.text: Estimate the causal impact of a digital financial literacy + mobile savings program ("Jipange") on savings behavior, income, and economic empowerment among women market vendors in Nairobi, Kenya — and identify which program components drive the effects.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: Kenya has one of the highest mobile money penetration rates in the world, yet women in informal markets remain largely excluded from formal financial services. Over 60% of women market vendors in Nairobi rely on informal lenders with predatory interest rates. Existing financial inclusion programs focus on access (opening accounts); evidence on comprehensive digital literacy + behavioral savings interventions for informal-sector women is limited. This study fills a critical gap: can an integrated program that combines digital skills, financial management training, and commitment savings devices shift women from informal to formal financial tools — and does that translate to higher savings and income? The staggered rollout and component analysis aim to answer not just "does it work" but "what works and for whom."

docs.title: Critical Success Factors
docs.text:
1. Market association trust and cooperation (especially across competing vendor groups)
  2. Training fidelity — trainer quality determines whether the program is delivered as designed
  3. Measurement of sensitive financial outcomes (income, savings, informal borrowing) with minimal bias
  4. Cohort enrollment and retention across all three waves
  5. Timely funding disbursements to prevent operational gaps

docs.title: Study Design at a Glance
docs.text: Element Detail Design Cluster RCT, market-cluster randomization, staggered rollout Location Nairobi, Kenya (Gikomba Market, Eastleigh, Kawangware) Sample ~1,200 women market vendors, 18 market clusters, 3 cohorts of ~400 Intervention "Jipange" — 12-week program: 4 weeks digital literacy, 4 weeks financial management, 4 weeks M-Pesa savings commitment device Control Waitlist (receives program in subsequent cohort phase) Randomization Market-cluster level, stratified by market area and baseline savings rates Primary outcomes Savings index, income index, financial empowerment index Secondary outcomes Financial stress (PSS-F adapted), digital skills proficiency, borrowing composition, business investment Measurement Baseline, midline (4 months post-randomization), endline (12 months), long-run follow-up (24 months, funding dependent) Pre-registration AEA Registry #AEARCTR-0000000 (registered Jan 2026, update pending for midline changes)

docs.title: Paper A1: "Measuring Financial Capability Among Informal-Sector Women: Validating an Adapted Assessment Tool"
docs.text:
Status: Data collection complete (Cohort 1 baseline + screening data). Analysis planned for Q3 2026.

Research question: How well does an adapted FINDEX-based financial capability assessment predict savings behavior and financial resilience among women market vendors in Nairobi, and which capability dimensions are most predictive?

Design: Predictive validity study using Cohort 1 screening scores (pre-randomization) to predict baseline savings behavior and M-Pesa transaction patterns. Compare performance to the original instruments validated in nationally representative samples.

Key data: - Screening scores for 380 Cohort 1 participants (adapted FINDEX assessment, 28 items) - Baseline self-reported savings and income (5-item index, with caveats about response rates) - Safaricom M-Pesa transaction summaries (pending data sharing agreement — Lisa following up) - Market-level economic data from the Kenya National Bureau of Statistics

Identification: Predictive validity (AUC, sensitivity, specificity). Machine learning comparison (LASSO, random forest) to assess whether the full instrument outperforms a reduced-form version.

Target journals: World Development, Journal of Development Economics

Collaborators: James Mwangi (lead author), Sarah Okonkwo, Lisa Park

docs.title: Paper A2: "Who Saves? Financial Behavior, Social Networks, and Market Context Among Nairobi's Women Vendors"
docs.text:
Status: Descriptive analysis pending. Awaiting Cohort 1 midline data for social network module.

Research question: What individual, household, and market-level factors predict savings behavior among women market vendors in Nairobi? How do social network structures differ between high-saving and low-saving vendors?

Design: Cross-sectional descriptive analysis using baseline + midline data. Social network analysis using the name generator module (midline instrument). Market-level analysis using cluster-level covariates.

Key data: - Baseline survey (demographics, household, financial behavior) — n=380 - Midline social network module (collecting Apr 2026) - Market mapping data (Faith's Cohort 3 prep work will generate cluster profiles) - Endorsement experiment item on attitudes toward formal financial services (midline)

Identification: Descriptive — conditional correlations, network statistics, market-cluster variation. Not causal.

Target journals: World Development, Economic Development and Cultural Change

Collaborators: James Mwangi, Faith Wambui, Sarah Okonkwo

docs.title: Paper B1 (Main Paper): "Digital Literacy, Savings, and Economic Empowerment: Experimental Evidence from Nairobi's Markets"
docs.text:
Status: Pre-registered. Cohort 1 program in progress. First results expected Q4 2026 (midline) with full results Q2 2027 (all cohorts, endline).

Research question: Does the Jipange program increase savings, income, and economic empowerment among women market vendors in Nairobi?

Design: Cluster RCT with staggered rollout across 3 cohorts. Market-cluster randomization (18 clusters: 9 treatment, 9 control per cohort pair). Intent-to-treat analysis with complier average causal effect estimates.

Primary outcome indices: 1. Savings index (M-Pesa balance, formal savings account, savings frequency) — measured via combined direct report + M-Pesa records + list experiment at midline/endline 2. Income index (weekly revenue, profit margin, business investment) — self-report + M-Pesa transaction volumes 3. Financial empowerment index (financial decision-making autonomy, reduced reliance on informal lenders, business planning)

Secondary outcomes: - Financial stress (adapted Perceived Stress Scale — Financial) - Digital skills proficiency (practical assessment) - Borrowing composition (formal vs. informal lending share) - Business investment (inventory, equipment, new product lines)

Power calculations (updated Feb 14, 2026): Using actual Cohort 1 ICC = 0.048 (baseline savings index): - 18 market clusters, ~67 individuals per cluster - MDE = 0.17 SD on savings index (80% power, 5% significance) - MDE = 0.15 SD with baseline covariate adjustment (R-squared ~0.25) - If Cohort 2+3 each reach 360 (minimum threshold), total N = 1,100 → MDE = 0.16 SD - Power adequate for main effects. Heterogeneity analysis (by financial capability level, age, market area) will be underpowered — pre-registered as exploratory.

Estimation strategy:

Primary specification: Y_ic = alpha + beta * Treatment_c + gamma * X_i + delta * Stratification_s + epsilon_ic

Where: - Y_ic is outcome for individual i in market cluster c - Treatment_c is cluster-level treatment assignment - X_i is a vector of baseline covariates (pre-registered: age, education, financial capability score, household size, years as vendor) - Stratification_s are randomization strata fixed effects (market area x baseline savings rate) - Standard errors clustered at market-cluster level

Robustness: - Lee bounds for attrition - Randomization inference (exact p-values given 18 clusters) - LASSO-selected controls (Belloni et al. 2014) - Cohort-specific estimates + pooled estimate with cohort fixed effects

Key threats to identification: 1. Spillover between treatment and control clusters (mitigation: market-cluster randomization with geographic buffers; monitoring via control group survey items) 2. Differential attrition (mitigation: intensive tracking, incentives, market association liaison network) 3. Measurement bias on sensitive financial outcomes (mitigation: list experiments, M-Pesa records, endorsement experiments) 4. Small number of clusters (mitigation: randomization inference, wild cluster bootstrap)

Target journals: American Economic Review, Quarterly Journal of Economics

Collaborators: Sarah Okonkwo (lead), James Mwangi, Lisa Park

Timeline: - Apr 2026: Cohort 1 midline → preliminary experimental results - Aug 2026: Cohort 1 endline - Dec 2026: Cohort 2 endline - Mar 2027: Cohort 3 endline - Q2 2027: Full pooled analysis - Q3 2027: Paper draft - Q4 2027: Circulate working paper

docs.title: Paper B2: "What Works? Unpacking the Components of a Financial Inclusion Program"
docs.text:
Status: Design phase. Depends on Paper B1 data + additional qualitative data.

Research question: Which components of the Jipange program (digital literacy, financial management training, M-Pesa savings commitment device) drive the effects on savings and income? Do effects vary by participant characteristics?

Design: Mediation analysis using the structured program components and intermediate outcomes. The 12-week program has three distinct phases: - Weeks 1-4: Digital literacy (smartphone skills, M-Pesa features, online price comparison) - Weeks 5-8: Financial management workshops (budgeting, profit calculation, savings goals, loan evaluation) - Weeks 9-12: M-Pesa savings commitment device (automated weekly transfers, goal-based savings, peer accountability groups)

The midline survey (administered at week 12) includes module-specific measures: - Digital literacy-related: smartphone proficiency test, M-Pesa feature usage, digital transaction frequency - Financial management-related: budgeting knowledge, profit margin calculation, savings goal specificity - Commitment device-related: savings consistency, peer group engagement, self-reported discipline

Identification: Causal mediation analysis (Imai et al. 2011). Cross-randomized design not feasible (components are sequential by design), so mediation estimates are suggestive, not definitive. Will use baseline-by-treatment interactions to identify who benefits most from which component (heterogeneous treatment effects).

Complementary qualitative evidence: James's qualitative interviews (n=40 treatment participants, purposive sampling across trainer groups and financial capability levels) will provide process evidence on which components participants found most valuable.

Target journals: Journal of Political Economy, Review of Economics and Statistics

Collaborators: Sarah Okonkwo (lead), James Mwangi

docs.title: Data and Measurement Notes
docs.text:
Administrative data status: - Safaricom (M-Pesa records): Data sharing agreement in negotiation. Lisa in contact with Safaricom's research partnerships team. Would provide anonymized transaction volumes, savings balances, and transfer patterns. Expected timeline: 2-3 months. - Kenya Revenue Authority: Formal business registration data for sample participants. Agreement signed. Data expected quarterly. - Kenya National Social Security Fund: Employment records (formal sector only). Inquiry sent, no response yet.

Survey instruments: - Baseline: 45 minutes. Fielded Dec 2025. Response rate 97%. - Midline: ~45 minutes (revised). Includes list experiments (new), endorsement experiment (new), social network module (new). Drops detailed time-use module. - Endline: ~50 minutes (planned). Full battery including all baseline items + experimental items from midline.

Measurement concerns (tracked): 1. Self-reported income and savings response rates (65-78% on sensitive items at baseline) → addressed via list experiments at midline 2. Item 14 refusal rate on screening tool → flagged for Cohort 3 revision 3. Item 22 possible social desirability bias → adding M-Pesa record cross-validation at midline 4. Control group attrition (1.6% at 4-week check — early but worth monitoring)

docs.title: Ethical Considerations
docs.text:
Adverse events protocol: Any participant reporting financial exploitation or coercion is referred to the Kenya Women's Enterprise Fund support line. Trainers trained in recognizing financial abuse indicators. Monthly adverse events review by James and the University of Nairobi ethics committee liaison.

Market association consent: In addition to individual informed consent, market-cluster consent obtained through association leader meetings in all 18 clusters. Process documented with signed agreements.

Data security: All personally identifiable information stored on encrypted IPA servers. Analysis datasets de-identified. SurveyCTO submissions encrypted in transit and at rest. Field tablets have remote wipe capability.

Waitlist control ethics: Control clusters receive the program in the subsequent cohort phase (4-month delay). No cluster permanently excluded. Staggered rollout justified by operational capacity constraints (cannot serve all 18 clusters simultaneously) and reviewed/approved by both IRBs.
